title: Correlated-exposure risk-gate check is permanently inert: paper_trade and analysis.risk disagree on every input key
evidence: Three independent shape mismatches across the cross-agent seam, each alone fatal: (1) _risk_gate builds candidate = {"symbol", "coin", "side", "notional_usd": ...} but correlated_exposure_check reads _finite(cand.get("notional")) -> None -> immediate early-return {"allowed": True, "reason": "no candidate notional -> nothing to gate"}; (2) the positions passed are HyperliquidPaperClient.account_state() rows keyed "coin"/"size"/"position_value" while the check reads pos.get("symbol") and pos.get("notional") -> every position is skipped, bucket_exposure always 0; (3) equity is never supplied (call is check_fn(positions, candidate, {}, cap_mult=2.0) with no equity= kwarg and no "equity" key in candidate) -> even with notional fixed it returns "no equity reference -> correlation cap skipped". Verified by executing _risk_gate with real account_state() shapes: a $50,000 BTC LONG on a $1,000 account holding $50k of open ETH/SOL exposure returns checks.correlated_exposure = {"passed": true, "allowed": true, "scaled_notional": 0.0, "reason": "no candidate notional -> nothing to gate"} and gate ok=True. The third leg of the advertised risk gate (DESIGN-SWARM "Journal + risk gate"; _risk_gate docstring check 3) never refuses or downsizes anything while reporting passed=true. The 530-test suite hides this by design: tests/test_journal.py's autouse fixture does monkeypatch.delattr(analysis_risk, "correlated_exposure_check") and the correlation tests install fakes accepting the paper_trade shapes, while tests/test_metrics_v2.py tests the real function only with "notional"-keyed dicts — the real seam is exercised nowhere.
suggested_fix: Align the contract on one side and add a true integration test. Easiest: in _risk_gate map the shapes the check expects — candidate = {"symbol": coin or symbol, "notional": float(notional_usd)}, positions mapped to [{"symbol": p["coin"], "notional": p["position_value"]} ...], and pass equity=float(equity) (the tradable_usdc already computed for the kill-switch). Alternatively make correlated_exposure_check accept notional_usd/position_value/coin aliases. Then add a test that runs _risk_gate against the REAL analysis.risk.correlated_exposure_check with account_state()-shaped positions and asserts a refusal (over cap) and a downsize (partial headroom).

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED by both code reading and a decisive runtime check with the venv python. All three claimed shape mismatches are real and verifiable in the staged code:

(1) Candidate key mismatch: C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/orchestration/paper_trade.py lines 311-316 build candidate = {"symbol", "coin", "side", "notional_usd"}, but C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/analysis/risk.py line 338 reads `notional = _finite(cand.get("notional"))`. Since "notional" is never present, the check ALWAYS hits the early return at risk.py:339-347: {"allowed": True, "reason": "no candidate notional -> nothing to gate"}. This alone makes the check permanently inert — legs 2 and 3 are never even reached.

(2) Positions key mismatch (latent, behind leg 1): account_state() rows are keyed "coin"/"size"/"position_value" (C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/execution/hyperliquid.py:258-261) while risk.py:370-371 reads pos.get("symbol") and pos.get("notional") — every position would be skipped even if leg 1 were fixed.

(3) Equity never supplied (latent, behind legs 1-2): the call at paper_trade.py:319 is `check_fn(positions, candidate, {}, cap_mult=2.0)` with no equity= kwarg and no "equity" key in candidate, so risk.py:356-364 would return "no equity reference -> correlation cap skipped".

Runtime proof (executed read-only with .venv python, temp MCP_CACHE_DIR): _risk_gate on a $1,000-equity account holding $50,000 of open ETH+SOL longs, candidate BTC LONG notional_usd=50,000, returns ok=True with checks.correlated_exposure = {"passed": true, "allowed": true, "scaled_notional": 0.0, "reason": "no candidate notional -> nothing to gate"}. Feeding the SAME scenario to the real correlated_exposure_check using its documented shapes returns {"allowed": false, "reason": "bucket BTC beta-notional 50000 >= cap 2000 -> refused (no headroom)"}. So the third advertised gate check (DESIGN-SWARM "Journal + risk gate"; _risk_gate docstring check 3) silently never refuses or downsizes anything, while reporting passed=true — a broken safety contract on a path that places live testnet orders.

The test-gap claim also verifies: tests/test_journal.py line 43 (autouse fixture) does `monkeypatch.delattr(analysis_risk, "correlated_exposure_check", raising=False)` for all journal tests, and the two correlation tests (lines 512-549) monkeypatch in fake lambdas that return canned verdicts without reading any candidate/position keys, so they pass regardless of shape. tests/test_metrics_v2.py exercises the real function only with "notional"-keyed dicts. The real cross-agent seam is exercised nowhere, which is why the 530-test suite is green. The live smoke test covered only the read pipeline, not order placement.

The suggested fix is correct: in _risk_gate map candidate to {"symbol": coin or symbol, "notional": float(notional_usd)}, map positions to [{"symbol": p["coin"], "notional": p["position_value"]}] (guarding None position_value), pass equity=float(equity) (the variable is already in scope at paper_trade.py:226-228; note the TypeError fallback at lines 320-322 retries without kwargs, so keep equity inside the candidate dict as well or the fallback path drops it), and add an integration test running _risk_gate against the REAL analysis.risk.correlated_exposure_check with account_state()-shaped positions asserting a refusal and a downsize.

title: Offline test suite now performs ~12 live HTTP fetches per run: gather_crypto_evidence gained four evidence tasks that test_gather_is_tolerant never patches
evidence: gather_crypto_evidence now adds "flow": cx.flow_metrics, "hl": cx.hl_context, "social": cx.social_scan, "news": cx.news_feed, but test_gather_is_tolerant monkeypatches only the original five seams (multi_timeframe, crypto_signals, derivatives, crypto_sentiment, crypto_ohlcv). Proven by instrumenting cx.get_registry with a spy while reproducing the test's patches: 8 registry fetches escape — taker_flow, top_trader_ratio, funding_history, crypto_ohlcv (Binance), hl_asset_ctx, hl_predicted_funding (Hyperliquid info), social_scan (Arctic Shift + StockTwits + 4chan + CoinGecko trending), news_feed (CoinTelegraph + CoinDesk + Google News). Measured: the single test takes 4.46s doing live HTTP (vs <5ms for true offline tests); on a no-network box each request rides the 20s httpx timeout. This breaks the repo contract "Tests are offline/no-network" (CLAUDE.md / conftest docstring) and, worse, hides seam regressions: the test never asserts anything about the new dossier keys, so any payload-shape break in the flow/hl/social/news seams (the highest-risk zone) still passes the suite — exactly the class of bug found in finding 1. There are also zero tests for the new server functions flow_metrics/orderflow/hl_context/social_scan/news_feed/market_pulse (grep of tests/ matches only the provider-level test_hl_info.py and test_social_news.py).
suggested_fix: In test_gather_is_tolerant, monkeypatch the four new cx functions too (or monkeypatch cx.get_registry with a stub raising/returning canned envelopes) and assert the new dossier keys carry the expected shapes. Add offline tests for servers/crypto.py flow_metrics() and orderflow() with a fake registry returning provider-shaped payloads (taker series buy_sell_ratio, top series ratio, long_short ratio, bars with taker_buy_volume, hl ctx mid_price/mark_price, l2book bids/asks) asserting each signal block computes — that test would also have locked the seam verified manually here.

verdict.reasoning: CONFIRMED by code reading and a decisive read-only reproduction. (1) The staged diff adds four tasks to gather_crypto_evidence (C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/orchestration/crypto.py:176-179: "flow": cx.flow_metrics, "hl": cx.hl_context, "social": cx.social_scan, "news": cx.news_feed), while tests/test_crypto_engine.py is NOT in the staged diff at all — test_gather_is_tolerant (lines 74-78) still patches only multi_timeframe, crypto_signals, derivatives, crypto_sentiment, crypto_ohlcv. (2) tests/conftest.py contains NO socket/network blocker — only env-var scrubbing, which is irrelevant because the new providers (Binance futures, Hyperliquid info, Reddit/StockTwits/4chan/CoinGecko, RSS) are keyless. (3) I reproduced the test's exact five monkeypatches under the venv python with a socket.getaddrinfo spy and a registry.fetch spy: 8 registry capabilities escaped the patches (taker_flow, top_trader_ratio, funding_history, crypto_ohlcv — fetched via the registry inside flow_metrics, so the cx.crypto_ohlcv patch does not intercept it — hl_asset_ctx, hl_predicted_funding, social_scan, news_feed), with live DNS/HTTP to 9 external hosts (fapi.binance.com, api.hyperliquid.xyz, arctic-shift.photon-reddit.com, api.stocktwits.com, a.4cdn.org, api.coingecko.com, cointelegraph.com, www.coindesk.com, news.google.com), elapsed 2.76s and returning real live market data (BTC mark_price 61225.0, real Reddit/news titles) — proving live HTTP, not cache. The claim's 4.46s vs my 2.76s is just network variance; both are orders of magnitude above a true offline test. This breaks the explicit repo contract ("Tests are offline/no-network" in CLAUDE.md; "contractually deterministic and OFFLINE" in tests/conftest.py docstring). The test still passes (its assertions cover only the patched mtf/signals keys), which is exactly why the 530-green suite hides it — and it equally hides any payload-shape regression in the new flow/hl/social/news dossier seams, since nothing asserts those keys. (4) The zero-server-level-coverage sub-claim also holds: grep of tests/ for flow_metrics|orderflow|hl_context|social_scan|news_feed|market_pulse matches only tests/test_social_news.py and tests/test_hl_info.py, and test_social_news.py imports only the provider classes (NewsRSSProvider, SocialPulseProvider) — no test imports makecrazypenny.servers.crypto's new functions. The suggested fix (patch the four new cx functions or stub cx.get_registry in test_gather_is_tolerant, assert the new dossier keys, and add offline server-layer tests with a fake registry) is correct.

title: open_manual silently skips journaling whenever the account pre-read is skipped or fails (gate off, reduce-only path aside, or transient account error)
evidence: account_before is fetched only inside `if not reduce_only and _gate_enabled():`. With MCP_SWARM_RISK_GATE=off, or when `_run(lambda: c.account_state())` returns {"error": ...} (gate is then skipped but the order still places), the subsequent journal call receives address=(account_before or {}).get("address") -> None, and _journal_record returns {"recorded": False, "skipped": "account address unknown - not journaled"} — the placed order never reaches decisions.jsonl. reconcile()/performance() can then never attribute the fill (it shows in account fills but as no journaled decision), contradicting the docstring promise "Placed orders gain a generated client order id (cloid) and a best-effort journal entry" and silently degrading the swarm's PnL attribution. Coupling journaling to an unrelated debug env knob is clearly unintended.
suggested_fix: Decouple journaling from the gate: always attempt one account_state() read (or derive the address from settings.hl_account_address / the signing key) before journaling, regardless of _gate_enabled()/reduce_only; only the gate evaluation should be conditional.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by direct execution (venv python, fake client through the client= seam): in C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/orchestration/paper_trade.py, open_manual fetches account_before only inside `if not reduce_only and _gate_enabled():` (line 434), and _journal_record (line 154) returns {"recorded": False, "skipped": "account address unknown - not journaled"} when address is None. Demonstrated: (a) with MCP_SWARM_RISK_GATE=off a placed risk-increasing manual order writes nothing to decisions.jsonl; (b) with a transient account_state() error the order STILL places (the risk gate is also silently bypassed) and is not journaled — this path needs no debug knob and is the strongest facet; (c) open_from_decision journals fine even with the gate off (it reads the account for sizing), so the manual path's gate/journal coupling is a structural accident, not policy. The 'intended design' defense fails at the cross-agent seam: _journal_record's docstring claims it skips to avoid 'unattributable rows', but journal.performance() explicitly includes address-None decisions for any account (`d.get("address") in (None, address)`), so a None-address row would have reconciled and attributed PnL correctly — the writer refuses what the consumer was built to accept. DESIGN-SWARM.md:166 promises journal append on open_manual with the only carve-out being append failure never blocking the order. Tests (test_journal.py) cover only the happy path (test_open_manual_success_is_journaled) and never assert journal state for gate-off/reduce-only/account-error, so the gap is invisible to the passing suite. Caveats: 'silently' is overstated — the return envelope does report {recorded: False, skipped: ...}; the data loss is in the journal file and downstream reconcile()/performance() attribution. And the reduce-only facet is plausibly by design (see corrected fix). Severity minor, as claimed: default config + healthy account journals correctly.
verdict.corrected_fix: The suggested fix is right for the gate-off and account-error paths but wrong for reduce_only. Do NOT journal reduce-only orders as decisions: close() never journals exits anywhere, and journaling a reduce-only SELL would create a direction=SHORT decision row that journal.reconcile() would mis-match as a new entry, corrupting round-trip PnL scoring. Correct fix: for risk-increasing orders (reduce_only=False), decouple the address from the gate — if account_before is missing or errored, resolve the address via one dedicated account_state() read or fall back to settings.hl_account_address (set in the documented agent-key setup); failing both, journal anyway with address=None, which performance() already attributes to the connected account (`d.get("address") in (None, address)`). Separately worth fixing while there: when account_state() errors, open_manual currently places the order with the risk gate silently bypassed — it should at minimum surface a gate-skipped warning in the envelope (or refuse, matching open_from_decision which returns placed=False on an account-read error).

title: social_pulse 5/min token bucket serializes crypto_screen deep dives and the CoinGecko keyless pool is double-spent across two independent buckets
evidence: Every decide_crypto now fetches social_scan (gather_crypto_evidence), and registry.fetch awaits bucket.acquire() BEFORE the cache lookup (TokenBucket.acquire blocks with no max_wait), so tokens are paid even on cache hits. A default crypto_screen (shortlist 10 -> 10 decide_crypto on distinct symbols, distinct cache keys) needs 10 tokens from a 5-capacity/5-per-min bucket: the last deep dive blocks ~60s purely in the social bucket — a hard latency regression in an existing tool with no error or marker explaining it. Separately, SocialPulseProvider._scan_trending GETs api.coingecko.com/search/trending under rate_key "social_pulse" while CoinGeckoProvider throttles under its own "coingecko" bucket; the module's own comment says the shared keyless pool is ~5 req/min per IP, so the two uncoordinated buckets can jointly exceed it, 429-ing the coingecko provider and tripping its circuit for crypto_global/crypto_quote (the screener/regime path).
suggested_fix: Route the trending call through the registry (a coingecko-served capability) or set the social provider's trending fetch to share rate_key="coingecko"; in the engine, consider fetching social evidence only for the final deep-dive candidates or checking the cache before paying bucket tokens (acquire after a cache probe), so screens do not serialize on the 5/min bucket.

verdict.reasoning: Verified, could not refute. (1) Latency serialization is real and demonstrable: the staged gather_crypto_evidence (orchestration/crypto.py) adds cx.social_scan(sym) to every decide_crypto; social_scan's chain is ["social_pulse"] only (core/config.py) and SocialPulseProvider has rate_per_min=5 with its own rate_key (social.py:232,237), giving TokenBucket(5) = 5-token burst + 1 token/12s refill. registry.fetch (registry.py:136-143) awaits bucket.acquire with NO max_wait BEFORE the cache lookup, so the wait is an unbounded silent sleep and tokens are paid even on cache hits. screen_crypto's default shortlist=10 is PER SIDE (crypto_screen.py:216-218), so up to 20 unique symbols are deep-dived with distinct social cache keys (all misses on a first screen): the deep-dive stage cannot complete faster than (N-5)*12s in the social bucket alone — >=60s extra at N=10 and >=180s at N=20 (the claim actually UNDERSTATES the worst case). I confirmed with a virtual-clock simulation of the real TokenBucket (20 concurrent acquires -> waits up to ~132s) and grepped orchestration/ for wait_for/timeout: none exist. Tests never see this (get_registry is monkeypatched, no real bucket) and a single-symbol smoke only consumes burst tokens — exactly the claimed test gap. (2) The CoinGecko double-spend is also real structurally: _scan_trending (social.py:429-435) GETs api.coingecko.com/api/v3/search/trending via the provider's own httpx client under the "social_pulse" bucket (5/min) while CoinGeckoProvider throttles separately under "coingecko" (10/min), so the two uncoordinated buckets jointly permit up to 15/min against a keyless pool social.py's own docstring measures at ~5 req/min; coingecko IS consumed in the same workflow (every decide's cx.crypto_sentiment() fetches crypto_global, chain ["coingecko"]), and 429s there record circuit failures (threshold 5, cooldown 60s). One inaccuracy: the claim says the tripped circuit hits "the screener/regime path", but crypto_regime uses crypto_ohlcv (binance) + crypto_sentiment (fear_greed), not coingecko — the impact lands on the per-decide sentiment block's crypto_global (cached 300s, so engine-side coingecko traffic is light) and host crypto_quote calls. That softens part 2's blast radius but does not refute the core findings. Severity "minor" is correctly calibrated: no wrong values, but a silent multi-minute latency cliff in an existing tool plus a self-documented upstream budget the integrated code can jointly violate.
verdict.corrected_fix: The suggested fix is directionally right with two corrections. (a) Latency: the highest-leverage fix is in the engine, not the registry — fetch social evidence once per screen (market-wide "CRYPTO" scan) or only for the final top_n candidates, rather than per deep-dive symbol; additionally/alternatively pass a bounded max_wait to bucket.acquire in registry.fetch (TokenBucket already supports it and raises RateLimited, which _fetch_data degrades to an {"_error": ...} marker) so a drained bucket degrades visibly instead of silently sleeping for minutes. Probing the cache before paying tokens also helps repeat calls but does NOT fix the first screen (distinct per-symbol keys are all cache misses). (b) Double-spend: "set the social provider's trending fetch to share rate_key='coingecko'" is not directly implementable as stated — rate_key is per-provider, not per-request, and pointing the whole social_pulse provider at the coingecko bucket would wrongly throttle its non-CoinGecko sub-sources. The correct version is to route trending through the registry as a coingecko-chained capability (or have SocialPulseProvider acquire from the registry's "coingecko" bucket just for the trending sub-fetch), so all api.coingecko.com traffic shares one budget. Note the consequence to advertise is the per-decide sentiment block (crypto_global) and host crypto_quote calls, not the regime path (crypto_regime uses binance klines + fear_greed).

title: journal.performance() silently reports every trade as unfilled (zero PnL, zero closed) when the fills read errors
evidence: `fills_resp = await _recent_fills(...)` returns {"error": ..., "disclaimer": ...} on any client failure (paper_trade._run converts exceptions), and the next line `fills = fills_resp.get("fills") if isinstance(fills_resp.get("fills"), list) else []` silently degrades that to an empty fill list. reconcile([], decisions) then marks every journaled decision "unfilled" and the scoreboard returns n_closed=0, hit_rate=None, total_realized_pnl=0.0 with NO error marker — wrong values presented as a clean result. The account-read failure two lines above is handled correctly (returns {"_error": acct["error"]}), so this is an inconsistent half of the same seam; a swarm cycle reading journal_performance after a transient fills hiccup would wrongly conclude its trades never filled.
suggested_fix: Mirror the account-read handling: if "error" in fills_resp (or "fills" is absent), return {"_error": fills_resp.get("error"), ...} or attach a "fills_error" marker and skip reconciliation rather than reconciling against an empty list.

verdict.reasoning: Verified by code reading and a live repro with the venv python. paper_trade._run (paper_trade.py:54-61) converts any client exception into {"error": ..., "disclaimer": ...} with no "fills" key; journal.performance (journal.py:558-559) then does `fills = fills_resp.get("fills") if isinstance(...) else []`, silently turning the error into an empty fill list. reconcile([], decisions) marks every journaled decision "unfilled" (rows are empty so cloid/oid/window matching all fail; journal.py:407-456). Repro with a fake client (account_state OK, recent_fills raising RuntimeError) returned {"n_decisions": 1, "n_unfilled": 1, "n_closed": 0, "hit_rate": null, "total_realized_pnl": 0} with zero error keys — wrong values presented as a clean scoreboard. The account-read failure two lines above IS handled ({"_error": acct["error"]}, journal.py:552-557), confirming the inconsistent seam. Test gap confirmed: tests/test_journal.py only covers the no-key path (account fails → "_error") and a happy-path FakeClient whose recent_fills always succeeds; no account-OK/fills-fail case. Severity "minor" is appropriate — needs a transient failure on the second of two independent network calls — but the misbehavior is real, demonstrable, and invisible to the passing suite.
verdict.corrected_fix: The suggested fix is essentially right; refine it to: after `fills_resp = await _recent_fills(...)`, treat `"error" in fills_resp` (equivalently, `"fills" not in fills_resp` — the success shape from HyperliquidPaperClient.recent_fills always includes "fills" as a list, even when empty) as a failure and return {"_error": fills_resp.get("error"), "n_decisions_journaled": len(read_decisions(settings=settings)), "disclaimer": DISCLAIMER}, mirroring the account-read branch at journal.py:552-557. Do NOT key on an empty list alone, since zero fills is a legitimate success state. Add a test: FakeClient with recent_fills raising → performance() returns "_error".

title: screen_crypto deep dive now serializes on the 5-req/min social_pulse token bucket — default screen gains ~3+ minutes of forced waiting
evidence: gather_crypto_evidence now fetches 'social': cx.social_scan(sym) per symbol (crypto.py:178), and crypto_screen.py:_deep_dive (crypto_screen.py:98-114, unchanged) runs the full decide_crypto per shortlisted candidate — with the defaults (DEFAULT_SHORTLIST=10 per side, also the MCP tool default at mcp_server.py:525) that is up to 20 deep dives. SocialPulseProvider.rate_per_min = 5 (providers/social.py:232 — deliberately capped to the CoinGecko trending pool), the registry awaits bucket.acquire() with NO max_wait (providers/registry.py:136-138), and TokenBucket.acquire sleeps indefinitely until tokens refill (providers/ratelimit.py:72-88). Cache keys include the symbol so 20 distinct symbols cannot share entries (TTL 120s is irrelevant). Result: burst capacity 5, then 1 token/12s -> the last social_scan waits ~(20-5)*12 = 180s, and because gather_crypto_evidence's asyncio.gather waits for ALL tasks, each affected candidate's entire decision (and the screen, and any paper_trade_decision queued behind the drained bucket) blocks on it. Pre-diff the deep dive was ~9 fast Binance/coingecko fetches per name and the whole screen completed in seconds. The 530 passing tests mock fetch points and the live smoke covered the single-symbol read path, so neither exercises 20-symbol bucket contention. Secondary cost: each per-symbol hl_asset_ctx is a full metaAndAssetCtxs universe download (hyperliquid_info.py:205-216), 20 of them per screen at TTL 30s. This also risks MCP-host tool timeouts on the crypto_screen tool the trade_swarm prompt tells the swarm to call every cycle.
suggested_fix: Give the screen a lighter dossier: add a flag to gather_crypto_evidence (e.g. include_social/news=False, or a profile='screen') and have _deep_dive use it — social_velocity is the smallest-weighted factor (0.5) and news is unscored, so the screen loses almost nothing. Alternatively fetch the market-wide social scan once per screen and reuse it, and/or pass max_wait to the bucket so social degrades to an {'_error': ...} marker instead of stalling the gather.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed real by direct code reading plus a scaled runtime simulation of the actual TokenBucket. The chain is exactly as claimed: screen_crypto deep-dives up to 20 unique symbols (DEFAULT_SHORTLIST=10 per side at crypto_screen.py:41, MCP default shortlist=10 at mcp_server.py:525; long/short candidate sets are disjoint), each via decide_crypto -> gather_crypto_evidence, which now fetches "social": cx.social_scan(sym) per symbol (orchestration/crypto.py:178). social_scan routes through the singleton registry (providers/__init__.py:60) to the sole-chain provider social_pulse (core/config.py:100) whose rate_per_min=5 (providers/social.py:232) builds a TokenBucket(5): burst capacity 5, refill 1 token/12s. registry.fetch awaits bucket.acquire() with NO max_wait and does so BEFORE the cache lookup (registry.py:136-143), and TokenBucket.acquire sleeps indefinitely without max_wait (ratelimit.py:72-88) — so no exception is ever raised for the tolerant gather to absorb; it just blocks. Cache keys include the symbol, so 20 symbols share nothing (and even warm-cache hits burn tokens, an aggravator). I ran a 12x-time-scaled simulation against the real TokenBucket with MAX_DEEP_CONCURRENCY=5 semaphore: 20 acquires -> first 5 instant, then strict serialization, last token at the exact scaled equivalent of 180s production wait; gather_crypto_evidence waits for ALL tasks, so each affected decision and the whole screen inherit it, and any concurrent paper_trade_decision queues behind the drained bucket. Refutation attempts failed: provider is keyless and always registered/supported; circuit breaker only trips on failures, not waits; the deep-dive semaphore adds no tokens. Secondary point also verified: _fetch_asset_ctx (hyperliquid_info.py:205-216) unconditionally re-downloads the full metaAndAssetCtxs universe per symbol (only the name map is memoized), 20 times per screen at TTL 30s — a cost, though not a blocking one (60/min bucket). Tests mock the get_registry/_fetch_data seams and the live smoke was single-symbol, so nothing exercises multi-symbol bucket contention. Minor nit in the claim's evidence only: pre-diff screens were not perfectly "seconds" — crypto_sentiment per deep dive already contended on the 10/min coingecko bucket (~60s worst case) — but the new 5/min social bucket is strictly tighter and additive, so the finding stands. The suggested fix is correct: exclude social/news from the screen dossier (social_velocity is the smallest-weighted factor at 0.5 and news is never read by score_crypto_evidence), or fetch one market-wide scan per screen, and/or pass max_wait so the fetch degrades to the {"_error": ...} marker the scorers already tolerate.

title: data_quality.coverage hardcodes the /4 norm, contradicting the conviction actually computed for crypto decisions
evidence: decide_from_scores builds data_quality with "coverage": round(_clamp(len(scored.get("categories", [])) / 4.0, 0.0, 1.0), 4) (debate.py:681) while _quant_conviction now uses the scorer-supplied norm: float(scored.get("category_norm", 4.0)) (debate.py:536-537), which is 5.0 for every crypto decision. Demonstrated: a funding-only crypto decision reports data_quality.coverage=0.25 while the conviction was computed with coverage 1/5=0.2. Every crypto TradeDecision (CLI, MCP tools, screen output, journal entries) now carries a coverage number that overstates the coverage actually used in its own conviction (e.g. 4 categories reports coverage 1.0 while conviction used 0.8) — a wrong value surfaced to consumers, though it feeds no downstream computation.
suggested_fix: In decide_from_scores, reuse the same norm: norm = float(scored.get("category_norm", 4.0)) or 4.0; "coverage": round(_clamp(len(scored.get("categories", [])) / norm, 0.0, 1.0), 4).

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED by reading the code and reproducing it live with the venv python.

Mechanics (all verified):
- Staged debate.py:533-537 changes _quant_conviction to use the scorer-supplied norm: `norm = float(scored.get("category_norm", 4.0)) or 4.0; coverage = _clamp(len(categories)/norm, ...)`.
- Staged crypto.py sets `_CATEGORY_NORM = 5.0` (line 72) and puts `"category_norm": _CATEGORY_NORM` into every crypto scored dict (line 380), which flows into decide_from_scores at crypto.py:492.
- debate.py:681 (pre-existing, untouched by the diff) still builds `data_quality["coverage"] = round(_clamp(len(scored.get("categories", []))/4.0, 0.0, 1.0), 4)` — hardcoded 4.

Live repro (read-only script via .venv python): a crypto-shaped scored dict with 4 non-neutral categories and category_norm=5 yields TradeDecision.conviction=0.8 (coverage factor 4/5=0.8) while data_quality["coverage"]=1.0; a funding-only dict reports coverage 0.25 while the conviction used 0.2. Exactly as claimed.

This is a genuine cross-agent-seam regression, not a definitional preference: pre-diff, both sites were the identical formula (/4) and were consistent for crypto; the diff updated one site and missed the other. The diff's own design intent says coverage's norm is scorer-defined — the new comment at debate.py:533 ("The scorer may carry its own coverage norm") and the staged CONTRACT.md §18.3 ("crypto scorer demands broader corroboration: coverage norm 5 (vs 4)"). So for crypto the surfaced transparency field now overstates the coverage the engine actually applied (e.g. reports 1.0 while the conviction shown beside it was discounted at 0.8). No test asserts data_quality.coverage, which is why the 530-test suite doesn't catch it.

However, the claim's blast-radius list is overstated, which is why severity stays minor:
- CLI: the crypto formatter `_format_crypto_decision` (main.py:438-445) prints only n_factors, NOT coverage; only the equity formatter (main.py:214) prints coverage, and equity is unaffected (norm stays 4 there — equity behavior is bit-identical, confirmed).
- Journal: journal.record_decision (journal.py:194-215) stores symbol/action/conviction/plan fields only — data_quality is not journaled.
- Actual surfacing: MCP tool JSON and crypto screen output, via TradeDecision.to_dict() which includes data_quality (core/types.py:632). Nothing computes from the value downstream.

The suggested fix is correct: in decide_from_scores reuse `norm = float(scored.get("category_norm", 4.0)) or 4.0` for the data_quality coverage, which preserves equity output exactly and makes the crypto field match the conviction's own arithmetic.

title: Correlated-exposure risk check is a silent no-op in production: gate passes a $1M order against $1k equity (cross-agent key-shape mismatch)
evidence: _risk_gate builds candidate = {"symbol", "coin", "side", "notional_usd": ...} and passes raw account_state positions ({"coin", "size", "position_value", ...}). But analysis/risk.py:338 reads cand.get("notional") (not notional_usd), risk.py:352 reads cand.get("equity") (never set, and the equity kwarg is never passed), and risk.py:370-371 read pos.get("symbol")/pos.get("notional") (account positions have neither). Executed: correlated_exposure_check(account_positions, {"symbol":"BTCUSDT","notional_usd":1_000_000}, {}, cap_mult=2.0) -> {'allowed': True, 'scaled_notional': 0.0, 'reason': 'no candidate notional -> nothing to gate'}; through pt._risk_gate with tradable_usdc=1000 the gate returns ok=True with checks.correlated_exposure = {'passed': True, ...}. The check can never refuse or downsize anything. Hidden by tests: tests/test_journal.py:43 deletes the real function in an autouse fixture and the correlation tests (test_journal.py:516-548) monkeypatch fakes, so the real seam is never exercised.
suggested_fix: In _risk_gate, build candidate = {"symbol": sym, "notional": float(notional_usd), "equity": float(equity)} (or pass equity= kwarg), and map positions to [{"symbol": p["coin"], "notional": p["position_value"]}] before calling correlated_exposure_check. Add one integration test that runs _risk_gate against the REAL analysis.risk.correlated_exposure_check (over-cap book must refuse; partial headroom must scale).

verdict.reasoning: Verified by reading both sides of the seam and executing the actual code with the venv python. Three independent key-shape mismatches, each individually fatal: (1) paper_trade.py:315 sets candidate["notional_usd"] but risk.py:338 reads cand.get("notional"), so correlated_exposure_check short-circuits at risk.py:339-347 with allowed=True / "no candidate notional -> nothing to gate" on every production call; (2) the equity kwarg is never passed at paper_trade.py:319 and the candidate has no "equity" key, so even with (1) fixed the check would skip with "no equity reference" at full notional (risk.py:350-364); (3) account positions from execution/hyperliquid.py:258-261 carry coin/size/position_value, but risk.py:370-371 reads pos["symbol"]/pos["notional"], so bucket_exposure would always be 0 even with (1)+(2) fixed. Live repro: pt._risk_gate(account with tradable_usdc=1000, $900k correlated book, notional_usd=1_000_000) returns ok=True with checks.correlated_exposure.passed=True and no scaled_notional (the no-op's scaled_notional=0.0 fails the scaled>0 condition at paper_trade.py:341, so no downsizing either). Counterfactual with correct keys refuses: "bucket BTC beta-notional 900000 >= cap 2000 -> refused (no headroom)". The other two gate checks do not cover this: the kill-switch needs realized daily loss and max-positions counts positions (2 < default 3), neither caps notional. Test gap confirmed: tests/test_journal.py:43 autouse fixture monkeypatch.delattr's the real correlated_exposure_check, and the correlation tests at lines 512-549 install lambda fakes whose return contract matches what _risk_gate consumes — the real cross-agent seam is never exercised, which is why all 530 tests pass. The check can never refuse or downsize anything in production; the suggested fix (map candidate to {"symbol", "notional", "equity"} or pass equity=, map positions to [{"symbol": p["coin"], "notional": p["position_value"]}], add an integration test against the real function) is correct, with one refinement: guard position_value being None when mapping.

title: Daily-loss kill-switch baseline is up to days stale: a >=5% loss yesterday blocks ALL of today, and a gain yesterday lets today lose ~20% unchecked
evidence: daily_loss_baseline always prefers the last snapshot <= today's UTC midnight, but snapshots are only ever written once per day, at the first gate evaluation (paper_trade.py:241-245). Executed: journal has one snapshot (yesterday 09:00 UTC, tradable_usdc=1000); today equity opens at 940 with ZERO trading today -> _risk_gate refuses (daily_loss_pct=6.0, reason '...refusing new risk-increasing orders until UTC midnight'), and a second call the same day still baselines 1000 (today's 940 snapshot is recorded but pre_midnight wins), so trading is blocked the entire day for yesterday's loss - contradicting both the docstring ('until UTC midnight') and DESIGN-SWARM.md ('compares today's realized+unrealized vs equity at UTC midnight'). Inverse hand-computation: day1 snapshot 1000, day1 gains to 1200; on day2 the baseline is still 1000, so equity can fall 1200 -> 950.01 (a 20.8% intraday loss) before the '5% daily' switch trips.
suggested_fix: Record an equity snapshot on EVERY gate evaluation (appends are cheap), and make the baseline 'equity at start of today': use the pre-midnight snapshot only if it is recent (e.g. within 24h of midnight / dated yesterday), otherwise fall back to the first snapshot of today. Add a test with now_ms on day N+1 over a single day-N snapshot asserting the gate passes when today's drawdown is 0.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading both sides of the seam and executing the real code. (1) Write cadence: snapshot_equity's only production call site is paper_trade.py:245, guarded by `latest.get("date") != today` (paper_trade.py:241-245), so at most one equity snapshot exists per UTC day, taken at the first gate evaluation of the day (any hour), even when the gate then refuses. (2) Baseline selection: journal.daily_loss_baseline (journal.py:348-355) unconditionally prefers the newest snapshot with ts_ms <= today's midnight; with the once-a-day cadence that is yesterday's first-evaluation snapshot, up to ~24h stale relative to actual midnight equity. Executed against the staged code: (a) sole snapshot yesterday 09:00 @ 1000, today equity 940 with zero trading today -> baseline pre_midnight 1000, loss_pct 6.0, gate refuses with 'until UTC midnight'; a later same-day call still baselines 1000 (today's recorded 940 snapshot loses to pre_midnight), so the entire day is blocked for yesterday's loss and only the *next* midnight (baseline becomes 940) lifts it — the refusal message and DESIGN-SWARM.md:170 ('compares today's ... vs equity at UTC midnight') are both violated; (b) inverse: snapshot 1000 yesterday morning, equity 1200 at yesterday's close, today falls to 950.01 (20.83% intraday drawdown) -> loss_pct 4.999 < 5.0, the '5% daily' switch does not trip. (3) Test gap: tests/test_journal.py:234 only uses snapshots 1-2h around midnight, an assumption the actual write policy never satisfies, which is why the 530-test suite stays green. This is a real cross-agent seam bug in an unattended-loop safety feature: wrong refusals for a full day in one direction, ~4x the permitted daily loss in the other. The suggested fix (snapshot on every gate evaluation; use the pre-midnight snapshot only when recent, else first-of-today; add a day-N+1-over-single-day-N-snapshot test) is correct.

title: reconcile() symbol+window fallback steals a later same-symbol decision's fill; the real decision reports 'unfilled' and PnL/R are attributed to the wrong (phantom) decision
evidence: The cloid tier can never fire for real fills (execution/hyperliquid.py:322-341 recent_fills rows carry no 'cloid' key and the generated cloid is never sent to the exchange - acknowledged in paper_trade.py:_new_cloid), so any decision with empty/unmatched order_ids falls to the 6h coin+side window. Executed: decision A (LONG BTC, order_ids=[], never filled - e.g. exchange-rejected or canceled limit) + decision B (LONG BTC 30min later, order_ids=[123]); the single BUY fill oid=123 belongs to B, but A is processed first (oldest-first) and window-claims it: A -> matched_by='window', outcome='win', realized_pnl=10.0, r_multiple=2.0 computed against A's stop; B -> 'unfilled'. journal_performance hit rate, by_symbol PnL, and R-multiples are all wrong. No test covers two same-symbol decisions (tests/test_journal.py:300-315 uses different coins).
suggested_fix: Skip the window fallback for decisions that DID record order_ids (an oid that matches no fill means genuinely unfilled); only window-match decisions with no recorded oids, and shrink the 6h default. Long-term, pass the generated cloid through exchange.order/market_open (SDK supports cloid=) and surface fills' 'cloid' in recent_fills so tier 1 actually works.

verdict.reasoning:
Attempted to refute; instead confirmed every factual predicate and reproduced the misattribution with the venv python.

1. Tier 1 (cloid) is dead for real fills: C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/execution/hyperliquid.py:327-340 builds recent_fills rows with only {coin, side, price, size, closed_pnl, fee, oid, time} — no 'cloid' key — and the generated cloid is never sent to the exchange (explicitly acknowledged in paper_trade.py:101-109 _new_cloid docstring: "today the execution client does not forward a client order id").

2. The window fallback (journal.py:444-453) runs whenever the cloid and oid tiers fail, including when the decision DID record order_ids that simply match no fill. There is no two-pass reservation: decisions are processed oldest-first and each claims fills greedily, so an older unfilled decision steals a fill whose oid is recorded on a later decision (the later decision's tier-2 match then fails on the 'i not in claimed' check at journal.py:441).

3. Repro (run against the staged code with .venv python): decision A (LONG BTC, order_ids=[999] unmatched, stop=95) + decision B (LONG BTC 30min later, order_ids=[123]); fills BUY oid=123 and SELL oid=124 (closed_pnl=10). Output: A matched_by=window, outcome=win, pnl=10.0, R=2.0 (computed against A's stop); B outcome=unfilled. Same result with A.order_ids=[]. So performance() (journal.py:566-601) reports wrong hit_rate, by_symbol PnL, and avg_R exactly as claimed.

4. Reachable in production: paper_open supports order_type="limit" (paper_trade.py:410), which rests a GTC order (hyperliquid.py:518-547); a resting (non-error) order IS journaled with its oid, and cancel (paper_trade.py:534) never touches the append-only journal — so canceled never-filled entries with dead oids persist. The 200-fill lookback in performance() also routinely leaves older decisions' oids unmatched. Same-coin same-side decisions within 6h are the swarm's normal operating mode.

5. Test gap confirmed: tests/test_journal.py:300-315 (test_reconcile_window_fallback_and_unfilled) uses different coins (BTC vs DOGE); no test exercises two same-coin same-side decisions, which is why the 530-test suite passes.
verdict.corrected_fix: The suggested fix (skip the window fallback for decisions that recorded order_ids; plumb cloid through exchange.order/market_open and surface fills' cloid in recent_fills) is correct but incomplete: my repro shows a decision journaled with order_ids=[] still window-steals a fill that a later decision's recorded oid identifies. The robust fix is a two-pass reconcile: pass 1 resolves all cloid/oid matches across ALL decisions and claims those fills; pass 2 applies the symbol+window fallback only to still-unmatched decisions against still-unclaimed fills (and additionally skip pass 2 for decisions that recorded oids, since an unmatched recorded oid means genuinely unfilled). Add a regression test with two same-coin same-side decisions where the second owns the only fill.

title: Exchange-rejected orders return placed: True and write a phantom journal row (order_ids=[]) - the direct feeder for the reconcile mis-attribution
evidence: placed = "error" not in order checks only the top level, but an exchange-level rejection (e.g. statuses=[{"error": "Order must have minimum value of $10"}]) is surfaced by _normalize_order_result NESTED at order["result"]["error"] (execution/hyperliquid.py:96-116); only transport/ExecutionError exceptions produce a top-level "error". Executed with a client returning that rejection shape: open_from_decision -> placed: True, journal recorded: True, journaled order_ids=[]. open_manual:468-470 has the identical check. The placed flag predates this diff, but the NEW journaling (paper_trade.py:674-684) now persists a decision row for an order the exchange refused; that row has no oids and becomes the phantom decision that window-steals the next real fill (previous finding). No test feeds a statuses-error order result through either path.
suggested_fix: Treat a nested result error as not placed: placed = "error" not in order and "error" not in (order.get("result") or {}); additionally skip _journal_record when _order_ids(order) is empty for a market entry. Add a test asserting a statuses=[{"error":...}] response yields placed: False and no journal row.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by executing the real code (venv python, fakes only at the documented _build_clients seam). (1) Structurally: open_position (execution/hyperliquid.py:552-562) nests the normalized order result under "result"; _normalize_order_result (96-116) places an exchange per-order rejection (statuses=[{"error": ...}], status "ok") at result["error"], never at the top level. Only _run's exception handler (paper_trade.py:54-61) creates a top-level "error". So placed = "error" not in order (paper_trade.py:658) and open_manual's `if "error" in res` (468) miss every exchange-level rejection. (2) Demonstrated: with the exchange returning {"status":"ok","response":{"data":{"statuses":[{"error":"Order must have minimum value of $10."}]}}}, open_from_decision returned placed: True, journal recorded: True; open_manual identically; decisions.jsonl gained rows with order_ids: []. (3) Feeder confirmed: feeding that phantom row plus a later real decision (order_ids=[777]) into journal.reconcile with the real fill (oid 777, side "BUY" as recent_fills normalizes) — the phantom matched via the 6h window fallback (matched_by: "window", outcome: "open") and the real decision came back "unfilled". Fills never carry the cloid (the execution client doesn't forward it, per _new_cloid's docstring), so the phantom can only ever window-steal. (4) Diff provenance matches the claim: "placed": placed / if placed: are context lines; the journaling inside the placed branch and all of journal.py are new. (5) Test gap confirmed: tests/test_execution.py's FakeExchange returns only filled/resting success shapes; no test anywhere feeds a statuses-error through either open path. Mitigating nuance only narrows the trigger, not the bug: the literal $10-minimum message is usually caught by the pre-flight _MIN_ORDER_USD check (raises -> placed False), but that check uses the pre-trade reference price, and exchange-side rejections (insufficient margin, price band/slippage, reduce-only violation) arrive in exactly this statuses-error shape — which _normalize_order_result was explicitly written to capture. The suggested fix is correct: also check the nested result dict for "error" (and skip journaling a market entry with no extracted oids), plus a test asserting a statuses-error response yields placed: False and no journal row.

title: open_manual is fail-open: a transient account-read failure bypasses the ENTIRE risk gate (kill-switch + position cap) and the placed order is silently never journaled
evidence: if "error" not in account_before: gate = _risk_gate(...) - on an account_state failure the branch is skipped and execution falls straight through to placement. Executed with account_state raising RuntimeError('info endpoint 503') while open_position works: result is placed: True, no 'risk_gate' key, journal {'recorded': False, 'skipped': 'account address unknown - not journaled'}. So in exactly the unattended-loop scenario the gate exists for (DESIGN-SWARM hard constraint 6), any Info-endpoint hiccup disables the daily-loss kill-switch and position cap for that order AND loses the journal row (silent data loss: the fill later reconciles to nothing or gets window-claimed by an older decision). open_from_decision is fail-closed for the same condition (paper_trade.py:595-596: returns placed: False 'could not reach testnet account'), so the two risk-increasing paths disagree.
suggested_fix: Mirror open_from_decision: when the gate is enabled and the pre-placement account read errors, return {placed: False, reason: 'could not reach testnet account', account: account_before} instead of placing ungated. (This also fixes the related journal gap: with MCP_SWARM_RISK_GATE=off, open_manual never reads the account, so NO manual order is ever journaled.)

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED by reading the staged code and executing it with the venv python (fake client, temp cache dir).

Mechanics (C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/orchestration/paper_trade.py): in open_manual, lines 434-452 read `if not reduce_only and _gate_enabled(): account_before = await _run(lambda: c.account_state()); if "error" not in account_before: gate = _risk_gate(...)`. When account_state fails, _run converts the exception to an error dict, the `if "error" not in` branch is skipped, gate stays None, and control falls straight through to line 453-467 where open_position is called. Line 494 then passes `address=(account_before or {}).get("address")` (None, the error dict has no address) into _journal_record, which returns {"recorded": False, "skipped": "account address unknown - not journaled"} (line 154-155).

Live demonstration (client whose account_state raises RuntimeError("info endpoint 503") while open_position succeeds): result was placed=True, NO "risk_gate" key, journal={'recorded': False, 'skipped': 'account address unknown - not journaled'} — byte-for-byte what the claim predicted. Control run with a healthy account_state: gate evaluated (ok=True) and journal recorded=True. Second claimed gap also confirmed: with MCP_SWARM_RISK_GATE=off, open_manual never reads the account, so a successfully placed manual order is never journaled (same skipped envelope), while open_from_decision always journals (it reads the account for sizing regardless of the gate).

This is a contract break, not a style preference: (1) open_manual's own docstring (lines 423-425) says risk-increasing orders "pass the same risk gate as the decision-driven path", and the module docstring (line 21-23) says "every risk-increasing placement first passes a risk gate" — falsified on this path; (2) open_from_decision is fail-closed for the identical condition (lines 594-596: returns placed=False, "could not reach testnet account"), so the two risk-increasing paths demonstrably disagree; (3) DESIGN-SWARM.md hard constraint 6 requires the gate + daily-loss kill-switch for unattended loops, and a transient Info-endpoint failure (a different endpoint from the exchange write path, so placement can still succeed) silently disengages both for that order; (4) the placed order leaves no journal row — silent data loss for reconcile/performance.

I tried to refute it as intentional design: the inline comment (lines 435-436) and the pre-existing test tests/test_paper_trade.py::test_manual_wrapper_converts_error_to_dict (Boom fake with no account_state, from commit 131d48b, untouched by this diff) show the author deliberately tolerated account_state failures — but only to avoid crashing on injected fakes; nothing documents "place ungated on account-read failure", and the function's own docstring claims the opposite (gate parity with the decision path). The _journal_record docstring does document skipping unattributable rows, but that makes the journal gap documented collateral of the gate bypass, not a justification for it. Tests cover gate refusal, gate-off bypass, and journaled success, but not the account-read-failure-with-working-placement seam — exactly the gap the smoke test also misses.
verdict.corrected_fix: The suggested fix is correct in direction (mirror open_from_decision: when not reduce_only and _gate_enabled() and "error" in account_before, return {"placed": False, "reason": "could not reach testnet account", "account": account_before, "disclaimer": DISCLAIMER} before placing), but it is incomplete as stated: the pre-existing test tests/test_paper_trade.py::test_manual_wrapper_converts_error_to_dict injects a client with NO account_state method, so under the fail-closed fix it would return the refusal envelope instead of the open_position error and the assertion `"error" in res` would fail. The fix must also update that test (e.g. give Boom an account_state, or assert the new refusal envelope) and add a regression test for the transient-account-failure path. Separately, deciding whether gate-off manual orders should still read the account just to journal (open_from_decision journals even with the gate off) is worth an explicit choice; as staged, MCP_SWARM_RISK_GATE=off means no manual order is ever journaled.

title: open_manual computes but ignores the gate's scaled_notional - the downsized order size is never applied on the manual path
evidence: open_from_decision applies the correlation downsizing (paper_trade.py:631-633: scaled = gate.get("scaled_notional"); if scaled: notional_usd = float(scaled)), but open_manual passes the original notional_usd straight to open_position after the gate; gate["scaled_notional"] is set on the gate dict (line 345) and only echoed in the response. Latent today because the correlated check is a no-op (finding 1), and untested - test_correlated_exposure_scales_notional only covers the decision path - but the moment finding 1 is fixed, manual orders will exceed the cluster cap while the response claims a scale was applied.
suggested_fix: After a passing gate in open_manual, apply gate.get("scaled_notional") to notional_usd when notional sizing is in use (and document that explicit size= sizing bypasses scaling, or refuse instead). Add a manual-path twin of test_correlated_exposure_scales_notional.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by both code reading and a targeted runtime check with the venv python. In C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/orchestration/paper_trade.py, _risk_gate sets gate["scaled_notional"] (line 345) when the correlated-exposure verdict carries a smaller notional, and its docstring (lines 198-199) promises "an allowed verdict may carry a scaled_notional that shrinks the order". open_from_decision honors that (lines 631-633: notional_usd = float(scaled)), but open_manual passes the original notional_usd straight to c.open_position (lines 453-467) and only echoes the gate dict in the response (lines 471-472). Demonstration: stubbing analysis.risk.correlated_exposure_check to return {"allowed": True, "scaled_notional": 100.0} (the exact seam and shape used by tests/test_journal.py:531 test_correlated_exposure_scales_notional), open_manual(notional_usd=500) placed the order at 500.0 while reporting risk_gate.scaled_notional=100.0; open_from_decision under the identical verdict placed at 100.0. The claim's caveats also verified: today the real check can never trigger scaling from the gate because _risk_gate builds the candidate with key "notional_usd" while correlated_exposure_check reads cand.get("notional") (risk.py:338) and no equity is passed - the real check returns scaled_notional=0.0 ("no candidate notional -> nothing to gate"), so the bug is latent until that separate wiring bug is fixed, at which point manual orders will exceed the cluster cap while the response claims a scale was applied. Test gap confirmed: only the decision path has a scaling test; no manual twin. Severity "minor/latent" is fairly stated; this is a genuine cross-agent seam inconsistency and response-contract violation, not a style nit.
verdict.corrected_fix: The suggested fix is correct and complete as-is: in open_manual, after a passing gate, apply scaled = gate.get("scaled_notional"); if scaled: notional_usd = float(scaled) before calling c.open_position, and add a manual-path twin of test_correlated_exposure_scales_notional. Note the explicit size= concern in the suggestion is already moot: _risk_gate only sets gate["scaled_notional"] when notional_usd is truthy (paper_trade.py:342), so scaling can never be set for size=-based manual orders and no extra refuse/document branch is strictly required (a comment noting this would suffice).

title: performance() silently reports every decision as 'unfilled' with 0 realized PnL when the fills read fails
evidence: fills = fills_resp.get("fills") if isinstance(fills_resp.get("fills"), list) else [] - when recent_fills returns {"error": ...} (ExecutionError/transient failure), fills becomes [] and reconcile marks every journaled decision 'unfilled', so the scoreboard shows n_unfilled=all, hit_rate=None, total_realized_pnl=0 with NO error marker. The account-read failure path IS surfaced (journal.py:552-557 returns {"_error": ...}), so the asymmetry makes a fills outage indistinguishable from 'no order ever filled' - the swarm prompt tells the model to act on this scoreboard each cycle.
suggested_fix: After fills_resp = await _recent_fills(...), if "error" in fills_resp (or "fills" missing) return {"_error": fills_resp["error"], ...} like the account path, or attach a "fills_error" marker and set n_unfilled stats to None instead of fabricating zeros.

verdict.reasoning:
Attempted to refute the claim but every premise verified against the actual code, and the misbehavior was reproduced offline with the venv python.

1. Error shape confirmed: `_run` in C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/orchestration/paper_trade.py (lines 54-61) converts ALL exceptions (ExecutionError and generic) into `{"error": str(exc), "disclaimer": ...}` and never raises, so `recent_fills()` returns a dict with NO "fills" key on any transient failure.

2. Independent failure is realistic, not hypothetical: `account_state` and `recent_fills` are two separate HTTP calls (`info.user_state` vs `info.user_fills` in execution/hyperliquid.py lines 244-247 and 322-325), so the fills call can fail (e.g. rate limit, network blip) right after the account call succeeded.

3. The asymmetry is exactly as claimed in journal.py: the account error path (lines 552-557) returns `{"_error": ...}`, but the fills error path (lines 558-559: `fills = fills_resp.get("fills") if isinstance(fills_resp.get("fills"), list) else []`) silently coerces the error dict to `[]` and proceeds to `reconcile`.

4. Reproduced: journaled one decision in a tmp cache dir, ran `performance()` with a fake client whose `account_state` succeeds and `recent_fills` raises RuntimeError("HTTP 429 rate limited"). Output: `n_unfilled: 1, n_closed: 0, hit_rate: null, total_realized_pnl: 0` with zero error markers anywhere in the payload — indistinguishable from "no order ever filled".

5. Downstream impact confirmed: the `trade_swarm` prompt (mcp_server.py lines 1315-1318 and 1341-1342) instructs the model to pull hit rate/lessons from `journal_performance` at step 0 of every cycle and to report PnL from it, so a fills outage feeds the trading model fabricated zeros.

6. Test gap confirmed: tests/test_journal.py covers only the happy path (`test_performance_aggregates`) and the account-failure `_error` path (`test_performance_without_key_returns_error_marker`); no test exercises account-success + fills-failure, which is why the 530-passing suite doesn't catch it.

Severity "minor" is fair — it is silent wrong data on the scoreboard, not a crash or a direct money-loss path (the daily-loss kill-switch baselines off equity snapshots, not off this reconciliation). The suggested fix (return `{"_error": ...}` like the account path, or attach a `fills_error` marker instead of fabricating unfilled/zero stats) is correct; the marker variant is slightly better since the account/positions data is still valid and usable.

title: Correlated-exposure risk gate is silently dead: cross-agent key-shape mismatch makes it a guaranteed no-op
evidence: _risk_gate builds candidate = {"symbol": symbol, "coin": coin, "side": ..., "notional_usd": float(notional_usd)...} (paper_trade.py:311-316) and calls check_fn(positions, candidate, {}, cap_mult=2.0) (line 319), but the real analysis/risk.correlated_exposure_check reads cand.get("notional") (risk.py:338), equity from the never-passed `equity=` kwarg or cand.get("equity") (risk.py:350-352), and positions rows via pos.get("symbol")/pos.get("notional") (risk.py:367-371) — while real account positions carry only coin/size/position_value (execution/hyperliquid.py:256-268). Live repro with production-shaped inputs (2 open positions, candidate notional_usd=50000 vs 1000 equity): correlated_exposure_check(...) -> {'allowed': True, 'scaled_notional': 0.0, 'reason': 'no candidate notional -> nothing to gate', 'bucket': None, 'cap': None}. So the BTC-beta 2x-equity cap documented as active in CONTRACT.md:1077-1079 and README.md:374-376 never refuses or downsizes anything. The 530-test suite cannot catch this: tests/test_journal.py:43 (autouse fixture) DELETES the real correlated_exposure_check, the gate tests at test_journal.py:512-549 install monkeypatched fakes, and tests/test_metrics_v2.py:423-459 tests the real function only with the keys it expects ('notional', 'equity'); no test runs the real function through the real gate with execution-shaped positions.
suggested_fix: In _risk_gate, translate to the check's contract: candidate = {"symbol": sym, "notional": float(notional_usd)} and pass equity (already in scope as account tradable_usdc/account_value) via check_fn(..., equity=float(equity)); map account positions to [{"symbol": p["coin"], "notional": abs(p.get("position_value") or 0.0)}] before the call. Also honor gate["scaled_notional"] in open_manual (only open_from_decision applies it, paper_trade.py:631-633). Add an integration test that exercises the REAL correlated_exposure_check through _risk_gate with execution-shaped positions and asserts a refusal/downsize.

verdict.reasoning: Attempted refutation failed; the claim is verified end-to-end. (1) Key mismatch is real: makecrazypenny/orchestration/paper_trade.py:311-316 builds the candidate with "notional_usd" but makecrazypenny/analysis/risk.py:338 reads cand.get("notional"), triggering the early no-op return at risk.py:339-347. (2) Equity is never supplied: the call at paper_trade.py:319 passes no equity= kwarg and the candidate has no "equity" key, so even with notional fixed the cap is skipped (risk.py:350-364). (3) Position shape mismatch: execution/hyperliquid.py account_state() emits coin/size/position_value while risk.py:370-371 reads pos["symbol"]/pos["notional"], so existing-bucket exposure would always be 0. Live repro with the venv python confirmed: gate-shaped inputs -> {'allowed': True, 'scaled_notional': 0.0, 'reason': 'no candidate notional -> nothing to gate'} even when the contract-shaped equivalent (bucket 20000 vs cap 2000) is a hard refusal; the 0.0 scaled_notional also never downsizes because paper_trade.py:338-344 requires scaled > 0. So the documented BTC-beta 2x-equity cap (CONTRACT.md §18.5, README.md ~374) can never refuse or downsize any order — a dead safety control on the live testnet write path. The test-gap explanation is accurate: tests/test_journal.py's autouse fixture monkeypatch.delattr's the real correlated_exposure_check, the gate correlation tests install fakes shaped like the gate's (wrong) call, and tests/test_metrics_v2.py:421-459 tests the real function only with its own keys; no test runs real-through-real with execution-shaped positions. Secondary point confirmed too: open_manual (paper_trade.py:439-452) ignores gate["scaled_notional"]; only open_from_decision applies it (631-633). The suggested fix is correct (translate keys, pass equity already in scope at paper_trade.py:226-228, map positions from coin/position_value, honor scaled_notional in open_manual, add a real integration test); one minor refinement is to use the coin as the symbol key on both candidate and positions so future non-empty betas lookups stay consistent.

title: open_manual fails open on account-read failure (order placed with NO risk gate and NO journal entry); README 'every placed order is journaled' is false
evidence: paper_trade.py:434-452: the gate runs only `if "error" not in account_before`; when the pre-trade account_state read errors (transient info-API failure in an unattended loop) the code falls through and still places the order at line 454 — bypassing the position cap, kill-switch, and correlated check entirely — and then _journal_record skips the entry because address is unknown (paper_trade.py:155: {"recorded": False, "skipped": "account address unknown - not journaled"}). The same unjournaled placement happens with MCP_SWARM_RISK_GATE=off (account never read). This contradicts open_from_decision, which refuses on the same failure (paper_trade.py:595-596, 'could not reach testnet account'), and README.md:370 which claims 'every placed order is journaled (JSONL under the cache dir) with a client order id'. Unjournaled entry fills can later be mis-claimed by another decision's symbol+time-window fallback in journal.reconcile (journal.py:444-453), corrupting PnL attribution.
suggested_fix: For risk-increasing manual orders, mirror open_from_decision: refuse with {"placed": False, "reason": "could not reach testnet account"} when the account read fails while the gate is enabled. For the gate-off path, still fetch the address (or take it from the order result) so the order is journaled; alternatively soften the README claim.

verdict.reasoning:
I tried to refute this and could not — every factual element checks out, and I reproduced the behavior live with the venv python.

DEMONSTRATED (executed open_manual against a client whose account_state raises and whose open_position succeeds, gate enabled): result was placed=True, no "risk_gate" key in the result, 1 order sent to the exchange, and journal={'recorded': False, 'skipped': 'account address unknown - not journaled'}. So on a transient account-read failure the manual path places the order with zero gate evaluation (position cap, kill-switch, correlated check all bypassed) and writes no journal row.

Code grounding:
- C:/Users/persi/Desktop/MCPenny/makecrazypenny/orchestration/paper_trade.py:434-452 — gate only runs inside `if "error" not in account_before:`; on error the code falls through to placement at line 454. `_run` (lines 54-61) converts any account_state failure into an {"error": ...} dict with no "address" key, so `_journal_record` at line 154-155 skips journaling.
- Gate-off path (MCP_SWARM_RISK_GATE=off): the whole block at 434 is skipped, account_before stays None, address None — same unjournaled placement, exactly as claimed.
- Contract breaks are real and STAGED IN THIS DIFF: README.md:370 ("every placed order is journaled ... with a client order id") and README.md:373-374 ("A risk gate runs before every order") are both new lines in `git diff --cached`, and both are false on this path. open_manual's own docstring (paper_trade.py:423-424) promises risk-increasing orders "pass the same risk gate as the decision-driven path" — but open_from_decision fails CLOSED on the identical failure (paper_trade.py:595-596 returns placed=False, "could not reach testnet account") while open_manual fails OPEN. DESIGN-SWARM.md line 44 makes the gate an unattended-loop-safety requirement, and `paper_open` (open_manual) is exposed to the host model in the /loop swarm flow.
- The downstream mis-attribution harm is plausible as stated: journal.py:444-453's fallback matches fills by coin+side+time-window only, and fills never carry the cloid (paper_trade.py:101-109 docstring: the execution client does not forward it), so an unjournaled entry fill on the same coin/side inside another decision's window can be claimed by it.

The only counter-evidence is that the fall-through is partially intentional: the comment at paper_trade.py:435-436 says an injected client without account_state should "degrade to a dict", and tests/test_paper_trade.py:200-206 depends on that (its Boom client has no account_state and the test expects the open_position error to surface). That explains WHY the code is shaped this way (a test seam) but does not make the production behavior correct — a deliberate degradation for injected test doubles became a fail-open safety gate plus silent journal loss for real transient API failures, contradicting the sibling function and the README written in the same diff. Tests cover gate refusal and happy-path journaling (tests/test_journal.py:607-630) but not this seam, matching the "what tests don't cover" risk profile. Severity "minor" is fair (testnet paper money, edge path), but the bug is real.
verdict.corrected_fix: The suggested fix is directionally right with one addition: when the gate is enabled and reduce_only=False, refuse on account-read failure exactly like open_from_decision (paper_trade.py:595-596), i.e. return {"placed": False, "reason": "could not reach testnet account", "account": account_before}; and for paths that skip the account read (gate off, reduce_only), recover the address afterwards (e.g. from the client/exchange wallet config or a post-placement account_state read) so the order is still journaled — or soften README.md:370/373. NOTE: the fail-closed change will break tests/test_paper_trade.py:200-206 (test_manual_wrapper_converts_error_to_dict), whose injected client has no account_state and currently relies on the fall-through to reach open_position's "no key configured" error; that test must be updated (e.g. give Boom an account_state that also raises ExecutionError and assert the refusal envelope, keeping the no-key UX informative).

title: swarm_goal_get / swarm_goal_set / journal_record tool outputs omit the DISCLAIMER (CONTRACT invariant 5)
evidence: CONTRACT.md:56: 'Any output that reaches a user carries the not-investment-advice disclaimer' (echoed in CLAUDE.md conventions). The three new MCP tools return journal_mod.goal_get() / goal_set() / record_cycle() verbatim (mcp_server.py:664-731), and none of those attach DISCLAIMER: goal_get returns {goal, updated_at} (journal.py:103-116), goal_set returns {goal, updated_at, stored} (journal.py:119-133), record_cycle returns the stamped entry (journal.py:220-237). Every sibling swarm tool does carry it (recent() journal.py:297, performance() journal.py:553/604, and all four data tools via servers/crypto.py). No test asserts disclaimers on these three outputs.
suggested_fix: Add "disclaimer": DISCLAIMER to the return dicts of goal_get, goal_set, and record_cycle in orchestration/journal.py (DISCLAIMER is already imported there), and extend tests to assert it.

verdict.reasoning: Verified empirically with the venv python: journal.goal_get() returns {goal, updated_at}, goal_set() returns {goal, updated_at, stored}, and record_cycle() returns the stamped entry — none carry 'disclaimer' — while recent() and performance() do (journal.py:297, 556, 604). The MCP tools swarm_goal_get/swarm_goal_set/journal_record (mcp_server.py, new §18 block) return these dicts verbatim through _dumps (plain json.dumps at mcp_server.py:87-89; no wrapper injects the disclaimer). CONTRACT.md:56-57 invariant 5 states 'Any output that reaches a user carries the not-investment-advice disclaimer from core/disclaimer.py', and the diff's own §18 opens with 'keeping every prior invariant' without exempting these tools — so this is a demonstrable contract violation, not a style preference. The convention is consistently honored everywhere else in the same diff (all four data tools in servers/crypto.py attach DISCLAIMER, journal_recent/journal_performance attach it) and pre-existing operational tools attach it too (execution/hyperliquid.py attaches it to open_orders/fills/cancel/set_leverage payloads and even error envelopes), confirming the omission is an oversight at the cross-agent seam rather than an intended exemption. Test gap also confirmed: tests/test_journal.py asserts 'disclaimer' on recent/performance/risk-gate refusals but never on goal_get/goal_set/record_cycle. Mitigating but non-refuting: pre-existing paper_pairs (core/crypto_universe.py:_hl_result) already omits the disclaimer, so the invariant had one prior deviation — that doesn't make the three new omissions contract-compliant. Severity minor is correct (no wrong values/crash/data loss; a broken documented invariant).
verdict.corrected_fix: The suggested fix is correct in substance, with one precision: attach DISCLAIMER to the RETURNED dicts only, not the persisted records. In goal_set, build the return as {**record, "stored": ok, "disclaimer": DISCLAIMER} rather than adding the key to `record` before tmp.write_text (otherwise the disclaimer string is persisted into goal.json). In record_cycle, add it to the `{**entry, "journaled": ok}` return, not to `entry` before _append_jsonl (otherwise every cycles.jsonl line stores the disclaimer text). In goal_get, add "disclaimer": DISCLAIMER to all three return dicts (both fallback paths and the success path). Extend tests/test_journal.py (and/or the MCP tool tests) to assert 'disclaimer' in each of the three outputs.
